params.text:
Staying safe on visits and de-escalating tension

Good personal safety is mostly about preparation and awareness. On the road, plan your route, park where you can leave easily, and keep valuables out of sight. On a visit, position yourself so you can reach the door, and keep your phone within reach.

If someone becomes agitated, calm de-escalation can prevent a situation from escalating into aggression.

  • Stay calm and speak slowly and quietly. Your own body language sets the tone.
  • Keep a comfortable distance and avoid standing over the person or blocking them in.
  • Listen, acknowledge how they feel, and avoid arguing or making demands.
  • Do not touch someone who is angry, and give them space and time.
  • Know your limits: de-escalation is about buying time to leave safely, not winning.

params.question: A relative becomes increasingly angry and starts shouting during a visit. Which response best supports de-escalation?

answers.text: Stay calm, keep your distance, listen and acknowledge how they feel while keeping your route to the door clear

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. A calm manner, space, active listening and a clear exit all help to defuse tension and keep you safe.

answers.text: Raise your own voice to show you are in control

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Matching their volume usually escalates the situation. A calm, quiet voice is far more effective.

answers.text: Move in close and put a reassuring hand on their shoulder

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Touching or crowding someone who is angry can feel threatening and provoke aggression. Keep a safe distance.

answers.text: Insist they calm down immediately and continue the task regardless

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Issuing demands and pressing on tends to inflame anger. Focus on defusing and, if needed, leaving safely.
